perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed a nd enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to its choice of law provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed a nd enforced in accordance with the laws of Outer Space, without regard to its choice of law provisions.
perturbation.explanation: The governing law clause now specifies 'Outer Space', which is not a recognized legal jurisdiction, rendering the clause unenforceable. This creates uncertainty about which laws govern the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1332 defines the requirements for diversity jurisdiction in federal courts, including the need for parties to be citizens of different states or citizens of a state and citizens or subjects of a foreign state. By specifying 'Outer Space' as the governing law, the contract attempts to operate outside of any recognized legal jurisdiction, and cannot be brought before the court due to lack of valid jurisdiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, except that the district courts shall not have original jurisdiction under this subsection of an action between citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state who are lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States and are domiciled in the same State; (3) citizens of different States and in which citizens or subjects of a foreign state are additional parties; and (4) a foreign state, defined in section 1603(a) of this title , as plaintiff and citizens of a State or of different States. (b) Except when express provision therefor is otherwise made in a statute of the United States, where the plaintiff who files the case originally in the Federal courts is finally adjudged to be entitled to recover less than the sum or value of $75,000, computed without regard to any setoff or counterclaim to which the defendant may be adjudged to be entitled, and exclusive of interest and costs, the district court may deny costs to the plaintiff and, in addition, may impose costs on the plaintiff. (c) For the purposes of this section and section 1441 of this title — (1) a corporation shall be deemed to be a citizen of every State and foreign state by which it has been incorporated and of the State or foreign state where it has its principal place of business, except that in any direct action against the insurer of a policy or contract of liability insura

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies that the contract shall be governed by the laws of 'Outer Space.' This is not a recognized legal jurisdiction. The original text specifies that the contract shall be governed by the laws of New York. This change creates a structural flaw because it renders the governing law clause unenforceable, contradicting the requirement that contracts must operate within recognized legal frameworks. While the provided law snippets do not directly address governing law clauses, the absence of 'Outer Space' as a valid jurisdiction implicitly contradicts the notion that a contract can be governed by such a law. Specifying an invalid jurisdiction makes it impossible to establish jurisdiction in a court of law, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party understands and agrees that money damage s would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that the other party s hall be entitled to seek injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or forestall any such br each or threatened breach. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other rights and remedies available at law or i n equity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party understands and agrees that money damage s would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement and that the other party s hall be entitled to seek injunctive or other equitable relief to remedy or forestall any such br each or threatened breach. Such remedy shall be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for any breach of this Agreement, and replaces all other rights and remedies available at law or i n equity.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows for both monetary damages and equitable relief (like injunctions). The modified clause states that equitable relief is the *exclusive* remedy, preventing the wronged party from seeking monetary compensation, which contradicts standard contract law principles allowing for a variety of remedies.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-305 states that remedies provided by the UCC should be liberally administered to put the aggrieved party in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed. By limiting the remedy to only equitable relief, the modified clause may prevent the aggrieved party from being fully compensated for their losses, thus contradicting the principle of liberal administration of remedies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-305. Remedies to be Liberally Administered. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-305. Remedies to be Liberally Administered. (a) The remedies provided by [the Uniform Commercial Code] must be liberally administered to the end that the aggrieved party may be put in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed but neither consequential or special damages nor penal damages may be had except as specifically provided in [the Uniform Commercial Code] or by other rule of law. (b) Any right or obligation declared by [the Uniform Commercial Code] is enforceable by action unless the provision declaring it specifies a different and limited effect. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by limiting the available remedy to only equitable relief, which directly conflicts with the principle that remedies should be liberally administered to put the aggrieved party in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states that "the remedies provided by [the Uniform Commercial Code] must be liberally administered to the end that the aggrieved party may be put in as good a position as if the other party had fully performed." The modification removes the possibility of monetary damages, potentially preventing the aggrieved party from being fully compensated, which undermines the law's intent.

perturbation.original_text: Vendor will take all reasonable, necessary, and appropriate measures, including encryption, to ensure that CVS Information stored on Vendor owned and secured devices cannot be accessed by una uthorized/inappropriate individuals.
perturbation.changed_text: Vendor will take all unreasonable, unnecessary, and inappropriate measures, including absence of encryption, to ensure that CVS Information stored on Vendor owned and secured devices can be accessed by una uthorized/inappropriate individuals.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires reasonable security measures. The modified clause mandates *unreasonable* and *inappropriate* measures that *allow* unauthorized access, which directly contradicts data security best practices and legal expectations for protecting confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for The Protection of Personal Information of Residents of The Commonwealth
perturbation.law_explanation: 201 CMR 17.00 sets forth standards for protecting the personal information of Massachusetts residents. Although Rhode Island law governs the contract, this law is relevant since vendor can have dealings with Massachusetts residents. The regulation mandates that businesses implement reasonable security measures to protect such information. The modified clause, by requiring unreasonable and inappropriate measures, directly violates this requirement, making vendor liable for any data breach caused by failure to encrypt data.

This page, 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth, is offered by Trial Court Law Libraries show more Massachusetts Court System This is a part of the law library : 201 CMR + This is a part of the law library : 201 CMR × Table of Contents 201 CMR 10.00: Weighing, sorting and culling of fish 201 CMR 11.00: New and used motor vehicle arbitration 201 CMR 12.00: Massachusetts do not call registry 201 CMR 14.00: Home Improvement Contractor Arbitration and Guaranty Fund 201 CMR 16.00: Placing, lifting and removal of security freezes 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth 201 CMR 18.00: Registration and enforcement of Home Improvement Contractor program 201 CMR 19.00: Customized wheelchair arbitration Regulation Regulation 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth Date: 11/13/2009 Organization: Office of Consumer Affairs and Business Regulation Regulatory Authority: MGL c. 93H Official Version: Purchase the official print version 201 CMR 17.00 establishes minimum standards to be met by persons who own or license personal information about a resident of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts in connection with the safeguarding of personal information contained in both paper and electronic records, to insure the security and confidentiality of customer information in a manner fully consistent with industry standards; protect against
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original by mandating 'unreasonable, unnecessary, and inappropriate measures' that *allow* unauthorized access to CVS Information, whereas the original text required reasonable measures to prevent such access. This contradicts 201 CMR 17.00, which establishes 'minimum standards' to 'insure the security and confidentiality of customer information in a manner fully consistent with industry standards; protect against' unauthorized access. The modification explicitly undermines this standard by requiring actions that facilitate unauthorized access, creating a clear legal contradiction as it promotes actions that directly violate the regulation's intent to protect personal information.

perturbation.original_text: Within ten (10) days following the receipt of a written request referencing this Agreement and this paragraph from either party furnishing Information hereunder, the receiving party will del iver to the furnishing party all tangible materials containing or embodying the Information received fr om the furnishing party, except for materials containing Information which has been incorporated into analyses, compilations, comparisons, studies or other documents prepared by the receivin g party or its representatives, together with a certificate executed by the receiving party certify ing that all such materials in the receiving party' s
perturbation.changed_text: Within ten (10) years following the receipt of a verbal request referencing this Agreement from either party furnishing Information hereunder, the receiving party will never del iver to the furnishing party any materials containing or embodying the Information, together with a certificate executed by the receiving party certify ing that none of such materials are in the receiving party' s
perturbation.explanation: Original: requires return of tangible materials within 10 days of a written request. Modified: changes 'ten (10) days' to 'ten (10) years', 'written request' to 'verbal request', 'will deliver' to 'will never deliver', requiring certification that *none* of the materials are in the receiving party's possession.  This effectively nullifies the obligation to return confidential information, drastically altering the original intent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act, 18 U.S. Code § 1836
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1836 protects trade secrets. Refusing to return tangible materials containing trade secrets, as the modified clause dictates, could be construed as misappropriation, especially if the receiving party continues to use the information for their own benefit after being requested to return it.  Delaying return for 10 years is also extremely unreasonable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Shane Greenstein | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Nov 4, 2013 Meet members of the 2013-2014 Berkman Community: Shane Greenstein, Niva Elkin-Koren, and Amy Johnson This week we are featuring our second installation of a series of interviews showcasing individuals in the 2013-2014 Berkman community. Conducted by our 2013 summer Berkterns, the…
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the intent of protecting trade secrets, potentially violating 18 U.S.C. § 1836. The original text requires the return of tangible materials containing information, while the changed text explicitly states that the receiving party will never deliver any such materials. This refusal to return materials, combined with the unreasonable delay of 10 years, as highlighted in the Law Explanation, increases the risk of misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'an owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action,' indicating the legal protection afforded to trade secrets and the consequences of their misappropriation. The change fundamentally alters the obligation regarding confidential information, making it a significant contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is irrelevant.
